meeting_purpose_blurb: Council makes official decisions in the form of ordinances, resolutions, or motions. Open to the public with public comment at designated times.
lead_headline: Arvada Kicks Off Wildfire Planning, Utility Rate Review in Dual Workshops

type_specific.meeting_summary: The Arvada City Council held two workshop discussions on May 12, 2026, with no formal votes taken beyond excusing an absent member. The first workshop covered wildfire mitigation and response strategies, including community preparedness, the recently completed Community Wildfire Protection Plan, the new Wildfire Resiliency Code, and public safety power shut-offs. The second workshop kicked off the annual utility rate and fee review process for 2027, covering water, wastewater, and stormwater infrastructure funding, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group.
type_specific.lead_headline: Arvada Kicks Off Wildfire Planning, Utility Rate Review in Dual Workshops
type_specific.meeting_purpose_blurb: Council makes official decisions in the form of ordinances, resolutions, or motions. Open to the public with public comment at designated times.

tonight_at_a_glance.synopsis: Council received briefings on wildfire preparedness strategies and utility infrastructure funding needs; no formal legislative votes were taken on either topic.
highlights: Fire and emergency management staff presented Arvada's wildfire mitigation framework, including the completed Community Wildfire Protection Plan and new Wildfire Resiliency Code
Staff outlined Xcel Energy public safety power shut-off (PSPS) response protocols, including generator deployment and county-coordinated charging stations
Utility staff launched the annual 2027 rate and fee workshop series, reviewing infrastructure investment needs and affordability programs
Council members discussed water conservation, tiered pricing fairness for large families, and tree canopy protection during drought
Council Member Davis was excused from the meeting by a 6–0 vote

presentations.topic: Wildfire Mitigation and Response Strategies Workshop
presentations.presenter_name: Brian (last name not stated in transcript) and Steven Parker
presentations.presenter_role: Emergency Management, City of Arvada; Arvada Fire Protection District
prose_paragraphs: City emergency management staff and Arvada Fire's Steven Parker jointly presented the city's wildfire preparedness framework, covering the February 2026 Community Wildfire Protection Plan (CWPP), the recently adopted Wildfire Resiliency Code, neighborhood outreach and home assessment programs, evacuation zone modeling, fuel mitigation projects (including a recent collaboration with Team Rubicon at Metobrook Park), prescribed fire considerations, Xcel Energy public safety power shut-off (PSPS) response protocols, and the ongoing Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Plan update. Staff acknowledged gaps in policy tools for incorporating evacuation analysis into development review and noted that the Jefferson County Sheriff's Office now leads the Mountain Metro Wildfire Mitigation Council, which coordinates monthly with regional fire agencies.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Steven Parker
presenter_quotes.text: a lot of people have the the misconception that, you know, we're looking at a a drought right now. And in drought conditions, obviously that wildfire wildfire threat goes up. But what I'm trying to show in in these pictures here is that even when we have wet winters, wet springs, and everything is green, we can still have wildfires.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: So, a lot of people have the the misconception that, you know, we're looking at a a drought right now. And in drought conditions, obviously that wildfire wildfire threat goes up. But what I'm trying to show in in these pictures here is that even when we have wet winters, wet springs, and everything is green, we can still have wildfires.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Unidentified
presenter_quotes.text: At the moment, we do not have a lot of policy tools to specifically account for evacuation needs in development proposals. That's something we feel should be addressed.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: At the moment, we do not have a lot of policy tools to specifically account for evacuation needs in development proposals. That's something we feel should be addressed. Um, when you look at Colorado Springs, they have an ordinance that actually specifically ties evacuation analysis into development proposals.

And so we have to work through that piece as well and balance, you know, how much do we put concern out there in the community with giving people time to prepare.

presentations.topic: 2027 Water, Wastewater, and Stormwater Rates and Fees Workshop (Kickoff)
presentations.presenter_name: Chris (last name not stated) and Jacqueline Gray
prose_paragraphs: Utility staff opened the annual workshop series for 2027 rates and fees, explaining the enterprise fund model, the difference between rates/fees and system development charges, and the city's 10-year financial planning approach. Staff reviewed the city's significant debt issuances ($50 million water and $50 million sewer in 2022; $90 million water and $15 million sewer in July 2025), affordability programs including a revised annual hardship credit and a new autopay option launched May 7 with over 1,000 sign-ups in one week, and a proposed ad hoc community advisory group to engage residents through 2030 on infrastructure investment decisions. Follow-up workshops are scheduled for July 28 and August 25, with first and second readings of rate adoptions planned for October.
